talent_tasks.task: Review the Extractor's output and determine the contract type and relevance to talent analysis. Identify all talent-related parties (performers, writers, directors, showrunners, composers, creators) and their roles. If the contract has no talent dimension (e.g., a pure content license between two corporations with no individual talent provisions), produce a minimal output noting this.

Assess guild and union compliance: Identify all guild/union references in the extraction (SAG-AFTRA, WGA, DGA, IATSE, AFM) Determine whether the production appears to be guild-signatory or non-signatory. If no guild is referenced, flag this as a threshold question. For each referenced guild, assess whether the contract terms meet applicable CBA minimums for: compensation, working conditions, credit, and residuals Identify any provisions that appear to fall below CBA minimums and classify as compliance violations Identify any provisions that exceed CBA minimums through individual negotiation and note the enhancement

Analyze residual and royalty structures: Identify all residual/royalty obligations in the extraction Determine which are guild-mandated vs. individually negotiated Who bears the residual payment obligation — the producer, the distributor, or allocated between them? How are residuals calculated? Against what revenue base? Does the contract attempt to offset guild residuals against other compensation (e.g., "all compensation inclusive of residuals")? If so, flag — this structure is contested and may violate CBA terms. Cross_reference to Financial Analyst for the economic waterfall position of residuals

Evaluate credit provisions: Screen credit: position (main titles, end titles, single card, shared card), size relative to title, and any conditions on credit Paid advertising credit: is the talent included in paid ads? In all paid ads or above a size threshold? Assess credit terms against applicable guild minimums. Note where the contract exceeds minimums through individual negotiation. Credit remedy: what happens if the credit obligation is breached? Is the remedy prospective correction only? Are there damages? Is injunctive relief waived? Credit in derivative works: does the credit obligation carry through to dubbed versions, clips, promotional materials, and digital distribution?

Analyze morals clause: What conduct triggers the clause? Is the trigger defined specifically or broadly? Who makes the determination that triggering conduct has occurred? What remedies are available — termination, suspension, financial clawback, or other? Is there a cure period? Can the talent contest the determination? Is the clause mutual (applies to both parties) or one-sided (applies only to talent)? Does the clause apply retroactively to pre-contract conduct? Assess the clause against talent-side best practices: narrow trigger, mutual application, cure period, and no retroactivity

Analyze key person provisions (if present): Who is the key person? What events trigger the clause — death, disability, unavailability, voluntary departure, breach? What are the consequences — termination right, suspension, replacement right, renegotiation? Does the key person have any right to cure or nominate a replacement? If the key person clause triggers termination, what happens to accrued compensation and credit?

Assess AI and digital likeness protections: Is there a grant of AI/digital likeness rights? What is its scope — the specific production only, or broader? Consent mechanic: is consent per-use (strongest), per-project, blanket for the term, or blanket in perpetuity (weakest)? Does AI-generated use of the talent's likeness, voice, or performance trigger additional compensation? Duration: do likeness rights extend beyond the term of the agreement? If so, for how long and under what conditions? Scope limitations: is AI use limited to the specific production, or can the likeness be used in other productions, marketing, or merchandising? Compare against the 2023 SAG-AFTRA CBA AI provisions as a baseline (for represented performers): does this contract meet, exceed, or fall below those protections? If the extraction shows AI/digital likeness provisions in the rights_grant but no corresponding protections in digital_and_emerging_rights, flag this asymmetry

Evaluate pay-or-play provisions (if present): What compensation is subject to the pay-or-play commitment — full fixed compensation, a reduced "play" amount, or contingent compensation? What exceptions exist — force majeure, budget contingency, financing conditions, production abandonment, disability, default by talent? Classify the commitment: genuine (minimal exceptions), conditional (material exceptions), or illusory (exceptions swallow the commitment) If talent is "played" (services used), what additional obligations attach (e.g., post-production services, press tour)? If talent is "paid" (services not used), what rights revert to talent (if any)?

Analyze exclusivity restrictions: Scope: what is restricted — competing projects in the same genre, all acting work, media appearances, commercial endorsements, social media activity? Duration: production period only, or extended before/after? How long are the extensions? Proportionality: is the exclusivity scope proportional to the compensation? Broad exclusivity with modest compensation is a finding. Carve-outs: are there exceptions for pre-existing commitments, personal social media, charitable appearances, or other standard carve-outs? Geographic scope: is the exclusivity worldwide or territory-limited?

Assess approval and consultation rights: Creative approval: does talent have approval over script changes, wardrobe, makeup, stunt doubles, body doubles? Marketing approval: does talent have approval over key art, trailers, taglines, and marketing use of their likeness? Approval standard: "not to be unreasonably withheld" (standard, meaningful) vs. "sole discretion of Producer" (nominal) vs. "mutual approval" (strongest) Consultation vs. approval: consultation is weaker — the producer must consult but can override. Distinguish clearly.

Evaluate the performer's grant of rights in their performance and likeness: What has the performer granted — rights in the performance only, or also in their name, likeness, voice, and biographical material? Is the grant limited to the production, or does it extend to merchandising, promotional use, and derivative works? Duration: is the likeness grant coterminous with the agreement, or does it survive termination (in perpetuity for the production)? Does the performer retain any reservation of rights in their own likeness for non-competing uses?

Evaluate termination protections from the talent perspective: If the production terminates early, what happens to the talent's accrued compensation? Do credit obligations survive termination? Does the performer's grant of rights in their performance survive termination (for footage already captured)? Is there a severance or kill fee if termination is not talent's fault? Are there any clawback provisions that could require talent to return compensation already received?

Identify modern talent protection gaps: Social media: are there mandatory posting requirements? If so, how onerous? Is the talent compensated for social media promotion? Publicity: are press tour and promotional appearance obligations defined and bounded (number of days, locations, expenses covered)? Data privacy: does the contract address the talent's personal data (contact info, biometric data for AI, location data)? Promotional clips: can the producer use clips of the talent's performance on social media indefinitely? Are there duration or platform limits?

Produce a talent findings summary: List all guild compliance issues (provisions below CBA minimums) List all talent protection gaps (absent provisions that should be present for this deal type) List all one-sided provisions favoring the producer/platform at the talent's expense List all strong protections — provisions that exceed industry norms in talent's favor Assess overall talent protection posture: strong, adequate, weak, or deficient Note cross-references to other specialist agents
Populate metadata and run verification before finalizing output.
